#' New sample projection
#'
#' Project one or more samples onto a subspace. This function takes a model fit and new observations, and projects them onto the
#' subspace defined by the model. This allows for the transformation of new data into the same lower-dimensional space as the original data.
#'
#' @param x The model fit, typically an object of class bi_projector or any other class that implements a project method
#' @param new_data A matrix or vector of new observations with the same number of columns as the original data. Rows represent observations and columns represent variables
#' @param ... Extra arguments to be passed to the specific project method for the object's class
#' @return A matrix or vector of the projected observations, where rows represent observations and columns represent the lower-dimensional space
#' @export
#' @family project
#' @seealso \code{\link{bi_projector}} for an example of a class that implements a project method
#' @examples
#' # Example with the bi_projector class
#' X <- matrix(rnorm(10*20), 10, 20)
#' svdfit <- svd(X)
#' p <- bi_projector(svdfit$v, s = svdfit$u %*% diag(svdfit$d), sdev=svdfit$d)
#'
#' # Project new_data onto the same subspace as the original data
#' new_data <- matrix(rnorm(5*20), 5, 20)
#' projected_data <- project(p, new_data)
project <- function(x, new_data, ...) UseMethod("project")



#' Partially project a new sample onto subspace
#'
#' Project a selected subset of column indices (`colind`) of `new_data` onto
#' the subspace defined by the model \code{x}. Optionally do a
#' ridge-regularized least-squares solve if columns are non-orthonormal.
#'
#' @param x The fitted model, e.g. `bi_projector`, that has a partial_project method.
#' @param new_data A numeric matrix (n x length(colind)) or vector, representing
#'   the observations to be projected.
#' @param colind A numeric vector of column indices in the original data space
#'   that correspond to \code{new_data}'s columns.
#' @param least_squares Logical; if TRUE (default), do a ridge-regularized solve.
#' @param lambda Numeric; ridge penalty (default 1e-6). Ignored if `least_squares=FALSE`.
#' @param ... Additional arguments passed to class-specific partial_project methods.
#'
#' @return A numeric matrix (n x d) of factor scores in the model's subspace, for
#'   those columns only.
#' @export
partial_project <- function(x, new_data, colind,
                            least_squares = TRUE,
                            lambda = 1e-6,
                            ...) {
  UseMethod("partial_project")
}




#' Construct a partial projector
#'
#' Create a new projector instance restricted to a subset of input columns. This function allows for the generation of
#' a new projection object that focuses only on the specified columns, enabling the projection of data using a limited
#' set of variables.
#'
#' @param x The original `projector` instance, typically an object of class `bi_projector` or any other class that implements a `partial_projector` method
#' @param colind A numeric vector of column indices to select in the projection matrix. These indices correspond to the variables used for the partial projector
#' @param ... Additional arguments passed to the underlying `partial_projector` method
#' @return A new `projector` instance, with the same class as the original object, that is restricted to the specified subset of input columns
#' @export
#' @seealso \code{\link{bi_projector}} for an example of a class that implements a `partial_projector` method
#' @examples
#' # Example with the bi_projector class
#' X <- matrix(rnorm(10*20), 10, 20)
#' svdfit <- svd(X)
#' p <- bi_projector(svdfit$v, s = svdfit$u %*% diag(svdfit$d), sdev=svdfit$d)
#'
#' # Create a partial projector using only the first 10 variables
#' colind <- 1:10
#' partial_p <- partial_projector(p, colind)
partial_projector <- function(x, colind, ...) UseMethod("partial_projector")
  


#' Project a single "block" of data onto the subspace
#'
#' When observations are concatenated into "blocks", it may be useful to project one block from the set.
#' This function facilitates the projection of a specific block of data onto a subspace. It is a
#' convenience method for multi-block fits and is equivalent to a "partial projection" where the
#' column indices are associated with a given block.
#'
#' @param x The model fit, typically an object of a class that implements a `project_block` method
#' @param new_data A matrix or vector of new observation(s) with the same number of columns as the original data
#' @param block An integer representing the block ID to select in the block projection matrix. This ID corresponds to the specific block of data to be projected
#' @param least_squares Logical. If `TRUE` use least squares projection.
#' @param ... Additional arguments passed to the underlying `project_block` method
#' @return A matrix or vector of the projected data for the specified block
#' @export
#' @family project
#' @seealso \code{\link{project}} for the generic projection function
project_block <- function(x, new_data, block, least_squares, ...) UseMethod("project_block")




#' Project one or more variables onto a subspace
#'
#' This function projects one or more variables onto a subspace. It is often called supplementary variable
#' projection and can be computed for a biorthogonal decomposition, such as Singular Value Decomposition (SVD).
#'
#' @param x The model fit, typically an object of a class that implements a `project_vars` method
#' @param new_data A matrix or vector of new observation(s) with the same number of rows as the original data
#' @param ... Additional arguments passed to the underlying `project_vars` method
#' @return A matrix or vector of the projected variables in the subspace
#' @export
#' @family project
#' @seealso \code{\link{project}} for the generic projection function for samples
project_vars <- function(x, new_data, ...) UseMethod("project_vars")


#' Transpose a model
#'
#' This function transposes a model by switching coefficients and scores. It is useful when you want to
#' reverse the roles of samples and variables in a model, especially in the context of dimensionality
#' reduction methods.
#'
#' @param x The model fit, typically an object of a class that implements a `transpose` method
#' @param ... Additional arguments passed to the underlying `transpose` method
#' @return A transposed model with coefficients and scores switched
#' @export
#' @family transpose
#' @seealso \code{\link{bi_projector}} for an example of a two-way mapping model that can be transposed
transpose <- function(x,...) UseMethod("transpose")



#' Reconstruct the data
#'
#' Reconstruct a data set from its (possibly) low-rank representation. This can be useful when analyzing
#' the impact of dimensionality reduction or when visualizing approximations of the original data.
#'
#' @param x The model fit, typically an object of a class that implements a `reconstruct` method
#' @param comp A vector of component indices to use in the reconstruction
#' @param rowind The row indices to reconstruct (optional). If not provided, all rows are used.
#' @param colind The column indices to reconstruct (optional). If not provided, all columns are used.
#' @param ... Additional arguments passed to the underlying `reconstruct` method
#' @return A reconstructed data set based on the selected components, rows, and columns
#' @export
#' @family reconstruct
#' @seealso \code{\link{bi_projector}} for an example of a two-way mapping model that can be reconstructed
reconstruct <- function(x, comp, rowind, colind, ...) UseMethod("reconstruct")



#' Reconstruct new data in a model's subspace
#'
#' This function takes a model (e.g., `projector` or `bi_projector`) and a new dataset,
#' and computes the rank-d approximation of the new data in the same subspace that
#' was defined by the model. In other words, we \strong{project} the new data into
#' the fitted subspace and then \strong{map it back} to the original dimensionality.
#'
#' Similar to \code{\link{reconstruct}} but operates on an external \code{new_data}
#' rather than the original fitted data. Often used to see how well the model's
#' subspace explains unseen data.
#'
#' @param x The fitted model object (e.g., \code{bi_projector}) that defines
#'   a subspace or factorization.
#' @param new_data A numeric matrix (or data frame) of shape
#'   \code{(n x p_full)} or possibly fewer columns if you allow partial reconstruction.
#' @param ... Additional arguments passed to the specific \code{reconstruct_new} method
#'   for the class of \code{x}.
#'
#' @return A numeric matrix (same number of rows as \code{new_data}, and typically
#'   the same number of columns if you're reconstructing fully) representing the
#'   rank-d approximation in the model's subspace.
#'
#' @export
#' @family reconstruct
#' @seealso \code{\link{reconstruct}} for reconstructing the original data in the model.
reconstruct_new <- function(x, new_data, ...) {
  UseMethod("reconstruct_new")
}



#' Transfer data from one domain/block to another via a latent space
#'
#' Convert between data representations in a multiblock or cross-decomposition
#' model by projecting the input `new_data` from the `from` domain/block
#' onto a latent space and then reconstructing it in the `to` domain/block.
#'
#' @param x The model fit, typically an object that implements a `transfer` method
#'   and ideally a `block_names` method.
#' @param new_data The data to transfer, typically matching the dimension of the `from` domain.
#' @param from Character string or index identifying the source domain/block.
#'   Must be present in `block_names(x)` if that method exists.
#' @param to Character string or index identifying the target domain/block.
#'   Must be present in `block_names(x)` if that method exists.
#' @param opts A list of optional arguments controlling the transfer process:
#'   \describe{
#'     \item{`cols`}{Optional numeric vector specifying column indices of the *target* 
#'       domain to reconstruct. If NULL (default), reconstructs all columns.}
#'     \item{`comps`}{Optional numeric vector specifying which latent components to use 
#'       for the projection/reconstruction. If NULL (default), uses all components.}
#'     \item{`ls_rr`}{Logical; if TRUE, use a ridge-regularized LS approach for the 
#'       initial projection from the `from` domain. Default FALSE.}
#'     \item{`lambda`}{Numeric ridge penalty (if `ls_rr=TRUE`). Default 1e-6.}
#'   }
#' @param ... Additional arguments passed to specific methods (discouraged, prefer `opts`).
#'
#' @return A matrix or data frame representing the transferred data in the `to` domain/block 
#'   (or a subset of columns/components if specified in `opts`).
#' @export
transfer <- function(x, new_data, from, to, opts = list(), ...) {
  UseMethod("transfer")
}

#' Default method for transfer
#' @param x Object
#' @param ... Ignored
#' @importFrom cli cli_abort
#' @export
#' @noRd
transfer.default <- function(x, ...) {
    cli::cli_abort(c(
      "!" = "{.fn transfer} is not implemented for {.cls {class(x)[1]}}.",
      "i" = "Provide a method or ensure the object inherits from a supported class."
      # Consider adding: "i" = "Supported classes include: cross_projector, ..."
    ))
}

#' Obtain residuals of a component model fit
#'
#' Calculate the residuals of a model after removing the effect of the first `ncomp` components.
#' This function is useful to assess the quality of the fit or to identify patterns that are not
#' captured by the model.

#' @param x The model fit object.
#' @param ncomp The number of components to factor out before calculating residuals.
#' @param xorig The original data matrix (X) used to fit the model.
#' @param ... Additional arguments passed to the method.
#' @return A matrix of residuals, with the same dimensions as the original data matrix.
#' @export
#' @family residuals
residuals <- function(x, ncomp, xorig, ...) UseMethod("residuals")


#' Retrieve the component scores
#'

#' data onto the components, which can be used for further analysis or visualization.

#' @return A matrix of factor scores, with rows corresponding to samples and columns to components.

scores <- function(x,...) UseMethod("scores")



#' Compute standardized component scores

#' the contributions of different components on the same scale, which can help in interpreting the results.
#'
#' @param x The model fit object.
#' @param ... Additional arguments passed to the method.
#' @return A matrix of standardized factor scores, with rows corresponding to samples and columns to components.
#' @export
#' @seealso \code{\link{scores}} for retrieving the original component scores.
std_scores <- function(x, ...) UseMethod("std_scores")

#' Extract the component matrix of a fit.

#' @param x the model fit

#' Shape of the Projector
#'
#' Get the input/output shape of the projector.
#'
#' This function retrieves the dimensions of the sample loadings matrix `v` in the form of a vector with two elements.
#' The first element is the number of rows in the `v` matrix, and the second element is the number of columns.

#' Inverse of the Component Matrix
#'
#' Return the inverse projection matrix, which can be used to map back to data space.
#' If the component matrix is orthogonal, then the inverse projection is the transpose of the component matrix.
#'
#' @param x The model fit.

inverse_projection <- function(x, ...) UseMethod("inverse_projection")


#' Partial Inverse Projection of a Columnwise Subset of Component Matrix

#' Even when the full component matrix is orthogonal, there is no guarantee that the partial component matrix is orthogonal.
#'

#' @param ... Additional arguments to be passed to the specific model implementation of `partial_inverse_projection`.
#' @return A matrix representing the partial inverse projection.
#' @export
partial_inverse_projection <- function(x, colind, ...) UseMethod("partial_inverse_projection")


#' Compose Two Projectors

#' @param ... Additional arguments to be passed to the specific model implementation of `compose_projector`.

compose_projector <- function(x,y,...) UseMethod("compose_projector")

#' Generic Permutation-Based Test
#'
#' This generic function implements a permutation-based test to assess the significance
#' of components or statistics in a fitted model. The actual procedure depends on
#' the method defined for the specific model class. Typical usage:
#'
#' \enumerate{
#'   \item Shuffle or permute the data in a way that breaks the structure of interest
#'         (e.g., shuffle labels for supervised methods, shuffle columns/rows for unsupervised).
#'   \item Re-fit or re-project the model on the permuted data. Depending on the class,
#'         this can be done via a \code{fit_fun} or a class-specific approach.
#'   \item Measure the statistic of interest (e.g., variance explained, classification accuracy, canonical correlation).
#'   \item Compare the distribution of permuted statistics to the observed statistic to compute an empirical p-value.
#' }
#'
#' S3 methods define the specific defaults and required signatures for the functions
#' involved in shuffling, fitting, and measuring.
#'
#' @name perm_test
#' @aliases perm_test perm_test.pca perm_test.cross_projector perm_test.discriminant_projector perm_test.multiblock_biprojector
#'
#' @usage perm_test(x, ...)
#'
#' @param x A fitted model object (e.g. \code{pca}, \code{cross_projector}, \code{discriminant_projector}, \code{multiblock_biprojector}).
#' @param X (Used by \code{pca}, \code{cross_projector}, \code{discriminant_projector}) The original primary data matrix used to fit \code{x}. Ignored by the \code{multiblock_biprojector} method.
#' @param Y (Used by \code{cross_projector}) The secondary data block (n x pY). Ignored by other methods.
#' @param Xlist (Used by \code{multiblock_biprojector} \[optional, default \code{NULL}\] and \code{multiblock_projector} \[required\]) List of data blocks.
#' @param nperm Integer number of permutations (Default: 1000 for PCA, 500 for multiblock methods, 100 otherwise).
#' @param measure_fun (Optional; Used by \code{pca}, \code{cross_projector}, \code{discriminant_projector}, \code{multiblock_projector}) A function for computing the statistic(s) of interest. Ignored by \code{multiblock_biprojector}. Signature/default varies by method (see Details).
#' @param shuffle_fun (Optional; Used by all methods) A function for permuting the data appropriately. Signature/default varies by method (see Details).
#' @param fit_fun (Optional; Used by \code{cross_projector}, \code{discriminant_projector}) A function for re-fitting a new model. Ignored by PCA and multiblock methods. Signature/default varies by method (see Details).
#' @param stepwise (Used by \code{pca}) Logical indicating if sequential testing (P3 projection) should be performed. Default \code{TRUE}. (The multiblock methods also perform sequential testing based on \code{alpha} and \code{comps}, but this argument is ignored). Ignored by other methods.
#' @param parallel (Used by all methods) Logical; if `TRUE`, attempt parallel execution via `future.apply::future_lapply`.
#' @param alternative (Used by all methods) Character string for the alternative hypothesis: "greater" (default), "less", or "two.sided".
#' @param alpha (Used by \code{pca}, \code{multiblock_biprojector}, \code{multiblock_projector}) Significance level for sequential stopping rule (default 0.05). Passed directly as a named argument to these methods.
#' @param comps (Used by \code{pca}, \code{multiblock_biprojector}, \code{multiblock_projector}) Maximum number of components to test sequentially (default 4). Passed directly as a named argument to these methods.
#' @param use_svd_solver (Used by \code{pca}) Optional string specifying the SVD solver (default "fast").
#' @param use_rspectra (Used by \code{multiblock_biprojector}) Logical indicating whether to use RSpectra for eigenvalue calculation (default \code{TRUE}). Passed directly as a named argument.
#' @param predict_method (Used by \code{discriminant_projector}) Prediction method (`"lda"` or `"euclid"`) used by the default measure function (default "lda").
#' @param ... Additional arguments passed down to `shuffle_fun` or `measure_fun` (if applicable).
#'   Note: For \code{multiblock} methods, \code{Xlist}, \code{comps}, \code{alpha}, and \code{use_rspectra} (for biprojector) are handled as direct named arguments, not via \code{...}.
#'
#' @details
#' This function provides a framework for permutation testing in various multivariate models.
#' The specific implementation details, default functions, and relevant arguments vary by method.
#'
#' \strong{PCA Method (`perm_test.pca`):} 
#' Relevant arguments: \code{X}, \code{nperm}, \code{measure_fun}, \code{shuffle_fun}, \code{stepwise}, \code{parallel}, \code{alternative}, \code{alpha}, \code{comps}, \code{use_svd_solver}, \code{...}. Assesses significance of variance explained by each PC (Vitale et al., 2017). Default statistic: F_a. Default shuffle: column-wise. Default uses P3 projection and sequential stopping with \code{alpha}.
#'
#' \strong{Cross Projector Method (`perm_test.cross_projector`):} 
#' Relevant arguments: \code{X}, \code{Y}, \code{nperm}, \code{measure_fun}, \code{shuffle_fun}, \code{fit_fun}, \code{parallel}, \code{alternative}, \code{...}. Tests the X-Y relationship. Default statistic: `x2y.mse`. Default shuffle: rows of Y. Default fit: `stats::cancor`.
#'
#' \strong{Discriminant Projector Method (`perm_test.discriminant_projector`):} 
#' Relevant arguments: \code{X}, \code{nperm}, \code{measure_fun}, \code{shuffle_fun}, \code{fit_fun}, \code{predict_method}, \code{parallel}, \code{alternative}, \code{...}. Tests class separation. Default statistic: prediction accuracy. Default shuffle: labels. Default fit: `MASS::lda`.
#'
#' \strong{Multiblock Bi-Projector Method (`perm_test.multiblock_biprojector`):} 
#' Relevant arguments: \code{Xlist} (optional), \code{nperm}, \code{shuffle_fun}, \code{parallel}, \code{alternative}, \code{alpha}, \code{comps}, \code{use_rspectra}, \code{...}. Tests consensus using fixed internal statistic (eigenvalue) on scores for each component. The statistic is the leading eigenvalue of the covariance matrix of block scores for a given component (T^T, where T columns are scores of block \emph{b} on component \emph{k}). By default, it shuffles rows within each block independently (either from \code{Xlist} if provided via \code{...}, or using the internally stored scores). It performs sequential testing for components specified by \code{comps} using the stopping rule defined by \code{alpha} (both passed via \code{...}).
#'
#' \strong{Multiblock Projector Method (`perm_test.multiblock_projector`):} 
#' Relevant arguments: \code{Xlist} (required), \code{nperm}, \code{measure_fun}, \code{shuffle_fun}, \code{parallel}, \code{alternative}, \code{alpha}, \code{comps}, \code{...}. Tests consensus using \code{measure_fun} (default: mean abs corr) on scores projected from \code{Xlist} using the original model \code{x}. Does not refit.
#'
#' @return
#' The structure of the return value depends on the method:
#' \describe{
#'   \item{\strong{`cross_projector`} and \strong{`discriminant_projector`}:}{
#'     Returns an object of class \code{perm_test}, a list containing: \code{statistic}, \code{perm_values}, \code{p.value}, \code{alternative}, \code{method}, \code{nperm}, \code{call}.}
#'   \item{\strong{`pca`}, \strong{`multiblock_biprojector`}, and \strong{`multiblock_projector`}:}{
#'     Returns an object inheriting from \code{perm_test} (classes \code{perm_test_pca}, \code{perm_test_multiblock}, or \code{perm_test} respectively for multiblock_projector), 
#'     a list containing: \code{component_results} (data frame with observed stat, pval, CIs per component), \code{perm_values} (matrix of permuted stats), \code{alpha} (if applicable), \code{alternative}, \code{method}, \code{nperm} (vector of successful permutations per component), \code{call}.}
#' }
#'
#' @references
#' Buja, A., & Eyuboglu, N. (1992). Remarks on parallel analysis. *Multivariate Behavioral Research*, 27(4), 509-540. (Relevant for PCA permutation concepts)
#'
#' Vitale, R., Westerhuis, J. A., Næs, T., Smilde, A. K., de Noord, O. E., & Ferrer, A. (2017).
#' Selecting the number of factors in principal component analysis by permutation testing—
#' Numerical and practical aspects. *Journal of Chemometrics*, 31(10), e2937.
#' \doi{10.1002/cem.2937} (Specific to `perm_test.pca`)
#'
#' @seealso \code{\link{pca}}, \code{\link{cross_projector}}, \code{\link{discriminant_projector}},
#'   \code{\link{multiblock_biprojector}},
#'   \code{\link{measure_interblock_transfer_error}}
#' @family perm_test
#'
#' @examples
#' # PCA Example
#' data(iris)
#' X_iris <- as.matrix(iris[,1:4])
#' mod_pca <- pca(X_iris, ncomp=4, preproc=center()) # Ensure centering
#'
#' # Test first 3 components sequentially (faster with more nperm)
#' # Ensure a future plan is set for parallel=TRUE, e.g., future::plan("multisession")
#' res_pca <- perm_test(mod_pca, X_iris, nperm=50, comps=3, parallel=FALSE)
#' print(res_pca)
#'
#' # PCA Example with row shuffling (tests different null hypothesis)
#' row_shuffle <- function(dat, ...) dat[sample(nrow(dat)), ]
#' res_pca_row <- perm_test(mod_pca, X_iris, nperm=50, comps=3,
#'                          shuffle_fun=row_shuffle, parallel=FALSE)
#' print(res_pca_row)
#'
#' \dontrun{
#' # Cross Projector Example (using cancor)
#' X <- as.matrix(iris[,1:2])
#' Y <- as.matrix(iris[,3:4])
#' ccr <- cancor(X, Y)
#' mod_cp <- cross_projector(ccr$xcoef, ccr$ycoef)
#'
#' # Perm test (is x2y.mse lower than chance?)
#' res_cp <- perm_test(mod_cp, X, Y=Y, nperm=50, alternative="less")
#' print(res_cp)
#'
#' # Discriminant Projector Example (using LDA)
#' library(MASS)
#' lda_fit <- lda(X_iris, grouping=iris$Species)
#' mod_dp <- discriminant_projector(
#'   v = lda_fit$scaling,
#'   s = X_iris %*% lda_fit$scaling,
#'   sdev = lda_fit$svd,
#'   labels = iris$Species,
#'   preproc = prep(center()), # Assuming center() was intended for LDA
#'   Sigma = lda_fit$covariance # Needed for LDA prediction method
#' )
#'
#' # Perm test (is accuracy higher than chance?)
#' res_dp <- perm_test(mod_dp, X_iris, nperm=50, alternative="greater")
#' print(res_dp)
#'
#' # Multiblock Bi-Projector Example
#' # (Requires a multiblock model 'mod_mb' from e.g. MFA or ComDim)
#' # Assuming 'mod_mb' exists and has 2 blocks:
#' # res_mb <- perm_test(mod_mb, nperm=50, comps=3) 
#' # print(res_mb)
#' # Example using provided Xlist (list of matrices X1, X2):
#' # X1 <- matrix(rnorm(50*10), 50, 10)
#' # X2 <- matrix(rnorm(50*15), 50, 15)
#' # Assume mod_mb was fit on cbind(X1, X2) with block_indices=list(1:10, 11:25)
#' # res_mb_xlist <- perm_test(mod_mb, Xlist=list(X1, X2), nperm=50, comps=3)
#' # print(res_mb_xlist)
#' }
#' @export
perm_test <- function(x, ...) {
  UseMethod("perm_test")
}



#' Screeplot for PCA
#'
#' Displays the variance explained by each principal component as a bar or line plot.
#' 
#' @param x A \code{pca} object.
#' @param ... extra args
#' @export
screeplot <- function(x, ...) UseMethod("screeplot")


#' Cross-validation Framework
#'
#' Generic function for performing cross-validation on various objects or data.
#' Specific methods should be implemented for different data types or model types.
#'
#' @param x The object to perform cross-validation on (e.g., data matrix, formula, model object).
#' @param folds A list defining the cross-validation folds, typically containing `train` and `test` indices for each fold.
#' @param ... Additional arguments passed to specific methods.
#'
#' @return The structure of the return value depends on the specific S3 method.
#'   Typically, it will be an object containing the results of the cross-validation,
#'   such as performance metrics per fold or aggregated metrics.
#'
#' @details
#' The specific implementation details, default functions, and relevant arguments vary by method.
#'
#' \strong{Bi-Projector Method (`cv.bi_projector`):}
#' Relevant arguments: \code{x}, \code{folds}, \code{max_comp}, \code{fit_fun},
#'   \code{measure}, \code{measure_fun}, \code{return_models}, \code{...}.
#'
#' This method performs cross-validation specifically for \code{bi_projector} models
#' (or models intended to be used like them, typically from unsupervised methods
#' like PCA or SVD). For each fold, it fits a single model using the training data
#' with the maximum number of components specified (\code{max_comp}). It then iterates
#' from 1 to \code{max_comp} components:
#' \enumerate{
#'   \item It truncates the full model to \code{k} components using \code{truncate()}. 
#'        (Requires a \code{truncate} method for the fitted model class).
#'   \item It reconstructs the held-out test data using the k-component truncated model
#'        via \code{reconstruct_new()}.
#'   \item It calculates reconstruction performance metrics (e.g., MSE, R2) by comparing
#'        the original test data to the reconstruction using the \code{measure} argument
#'        or a custom \code{measure_fun}.
#' }
#' The \code{fit_fun} must accept an argument \code{ncomp}. Additional arguments in \code{...}
#' are passed to \code{fit_fun} and \code{measure_fun}.
#'
#' The return value is a \code{cv_fit} object (a list with class `cv_fit`), where the
#' \code{$results} element is a tibble. Each row corresponds to a fold, containing
#' the fold index (\code{fold}) and a nested tibble (\code{component_metrics}).
#' The \code{component_metrics} tibble has rows for each component evaluated (1 to
#' \code{max_comp}) and columns for the component index (\code{comp}) plus all
#' calculated metrics (e.g., \code{mse}, \code{r2}, \code{mae}) or error messages
#' (\code{comp_error}). If \code{return_models=TRUE}, the full model fitted on the training
#' data for each fold is included in a list column \code{model_full}.
#'
#' @export
#' @family cv
#' @seealso \code{\link{cv_generic}}
cv <- function(x, folds, ...) {
  UseMethod("cv")
}


#' Identify Original Variables Used by a Projector
#' 
#' Determines which columns from the *original* input space contribute 
#' (have non-zero influence) to *any* of the output components of the projector.
#' 
#' @param x A projector object (e.g., `projector`, `composed_projector`).
#' @param ... Additional arguments passed to specific methods, such as:
#'   \describe{
#'     \item{`tol`}{Numeric tolerance for determining non-zero coefficients. Default is 1e-8 for some methods.}
#'   }
#' 
#' @return A sorted numeric vector of unique indices corresponding to the original input variables.
#' @export
variables_used <- function(x, ...) {
    UseMethod("variables_used")
}

#' Identify Original Variables for a Specific Component
#' 
#' Determines which columns from the *original* input space contribute 
#' (have non-zero influence) to a *specific* output component of the projector.
#' 
#' @param x A projector object (e.g., `projector`, `composed_projector`).
#' @param k The index of the output component to query.
#' @param ... Additional arguments passed to specific methods, such as:
#'   \describe{
#'     \item{`tol`}{Numeric tolerance for determining non-zero coefficients. Default is 1e-8 for some methods.}
#'   }
#' 
#' @return A sorted numeric vector of unique indices corresponding to the original input variables.
#' @export
vars_for_component <- function(x, k, ...) {
    UseMethod("vars_for_component")
}

#' @noRd
split_matrix <- function(X, fac) {
  idx <- split(1:nrow(X), fac)
  lapply(idx, function(i) X[i,])
}


#' Compute column-wise mean in X for each factor level of Y
#'
#' This function computes group means for each factor level of Y in the provided data matrix X.
#'
#' @param Y a vector of labels to compute means over disjoint sets
#' @param X a data matrix from which to compute means
#' @return a matrix with row names corresponding to factor levels of Y and column-wise means for each factor level
#' @export
#' @examples
#' # Example data
#' X <- matrix(rnorm(50), 10, 5)
#' Y <- factor(rep(1:2, each = 5))
#'
#' # Compute group means
#' gm <- group_means(Y, X)
group_means <- function (Y, X) {
  chk::chk_equal(nrow(X), length(Y))
  
  if (all(table(Y) == 1)) {
    warnings("`Y` does not contain more than one replicate of any level")
    row.names(X) <- names(table(Y))
    X
  }
  else {
    if (any(is.na(X))) {
      xspl <- split_matrix(X, Y)
      ret <- do.call(rbind, lapply(xspl, function(x) matrixStats::colMeans2(x, na.rm = TRUE)))
      row.names(ret) <- names(xspl)
      ret
    }
    else {
      Rs <- rowsum(X, Y, na.rm = TRUE)
      yt <- table(Y)
      ret <- sweep(Rs, 1, yt, "/")
      row.names(ret) <- names(yt)
      ret
    }
  }
}

content.text:
Based on my comprehensive analysis of the core R files in the multivarious package, here's a structured summary of their functionality and complexity:

## Core R Files Analysis Summary

### 1. `/Users/bbuchsbaum/code/multivarious/R/projector.R` - Core Projector Functionality
**Main Classes/Structures:**
- `projector` - Base class for dimensionality reduction
- `partial_projector` - Subset-based projector

**Key Exported Functions:**
- `projector()` - Constructor for projector objects
- `project()`, `partial_project()`, `reconstruct_new()` - Core projection methods
- `truncate()`, `reprocess()` - Model manipulation
- `inverse_projection()`, `partial_inverse_projection()` - Inverse operations

**Dependencies:**
- chk, assertthat, corpcor, crayon

**Complexity Level:** **High**
- Complex caching system with environment-based storage
- Multiple projection modes (full, partial, least squares)
- Sophisticated inverse projection calculations
- Error handling and validation throughout

**Core Algorithms:**
- Matrix projections: `X %*% v`
- Pseudoinverse calculations via corpcor
- Ridge-regularized least squares solutions

### 2. `/Users/bbuchsbaum/code/multivarious/R/bi_projector.R` - Bidirectional Projector
**Main Classes/Structures:**
- `bi_projector` - Two-way mapping class (inherits from projector)

**Key Exported Functions:**
- `bi_projector()` - Constructor
- `scores()`, `sdev()` - Score and deviation accessors
- `project_vars()` - Variable projection
- `reconstruct()`, `residuals()` - Reconstruction methods

**Dependencies:**
- Inherits projector dependencies
- Matrix operations

**Complexity Level:** **Medium-High**
- Builds on projector complexity
- Handles both sample and variable projections
- Score-based reconstruction algorithms

**Core Algorithms:**
- SVD-based projections
- Variable space projections: `t(new_data) %*% scores %*% diag(1/variance)`

### 3. `/Users/bbuchsbaum/code/multivarious/R/pca.R` - PCA Implementation
**Main Classes/Structures:**
- `pca` - PCA class (inherits from bi_projector)
- `rotated_pca` - Rotated PCA variant
- `perm_test_pca` - Permutation test results

**Key Exported Functions:**
- `pca()` - Main PCA function
- `perm_test.pca()` - Permutation testing (highly complex)
- `rotate.pca()` - Component rotation
- `biplot.pca()` - Enhanced visualization
- `pca_outliers()` - Outlier detection

**Dependencies:**
- GPArotation, ggplot2, ggrepel, future.apply, RSpectra, tibble, dplyr

**Complexity Level:** **High**
- Most complex single file in the package
- Advanced permutation testing with P3 projection
- Statistical rotation methods (varimax, promax, etc.)
- Complex visualization with ggrepel
- Sequential hypothesis testing

**Core Algorithms:**
- SVD via `svd_wrapper()`
- Permutation testing with Vitale et al. (2017) methodology
- Factor rotation via GPArotation
- Hotelling T² and Q-residual calculations

### 4. `/Users/bbuchsbaum/code/multivarious/R/svd.R` - SVD Wrapper
**Main Classes/Structures:**
- `svd` - SVD results class

**Key Exported Functions:**
- `svd_wrapper()` - Multi-method SVD interface
- `std_scores.svd()` - Standardized scores

**Dependencies:**
- RSpectra, rsvd, irlba, corpcor, svd

**Complexity Level:** **Medium**
- Method dispatch for multiple SVD algorithms
- Tolerance-based singular value filtering
- Error handling for different SVD packages

**Core Algorithms:**
- Multiple SVD implementations: base, fast, irlba, propack, rsvd, svds
- Relative tolerance filtering: `res$d > tol * res$d[1]`

### 5. `/Users/bbuchsbaum/code/multivarious/R/multiblock.R` - Multiblock Analysis
**Main Classes/Structures:**
- `multiblock_projector` - Multi-block projector
- `multiblock_biprojector` - Two-way multiblock projector
- `perm_test_multiblock` - Permutation test results

**Key Exported Functions:**
- `multiblock_projector()`, `multiblock_biprojector()` - Constructors
- `project_block()` - Block-specific projection
- `perm_test.multiblock_biprojector()` - Complex consensus testing
- `perm_test.multiblock_projector()` - Score-based consensus testing

**Dependencies:**
- RSpectra, future.apply, stats

**Complexity Level:** **High**
- Complex block index management
- Advanced consensus testing algorithms
- Sequential component testing with stopping rules
- Eigenvalue-based statistics

**Core Algorithms:**
- Block-wise projections
- Consensus testing via eigenvalue analysis: `RSpectra::svds(S, k=1)$d[1]`
- Cross-block correlation analysis

### 6. `/Users/bbuchsbaum/code/multivarious/R/pre_process.R` - Preprocessing Functionality
**Main Classes/Structures:**
- `prepper` - Pipeline builder
- `pre_processor` - Finalized processor
- `concat_pre_processor` - Block-wise processor

**Key Exported Functions:**
- `pass()`, `center()`, `colscale()`, `standardize()` - Preprocessing steps
- `prep()` - Pipeline finalizer (deprecated)
- `concat_pre_processors()` - Block concatenation
- Modern API: `fit()`, `fit_transform()`, `transform()`, `inverse_transform()`

**Dependencies:**
- rlang, Matrix, matrixStats, chk, lifecycle

**Complexity Level:** **Medium-High**
- Environment-based parameter storage
- Complex block-wise processing
- API transition (deprecated → modern)
- Sophisticated column index mapping

**Core Algorithms:**
- Centering: `sweep(X, 2, colMeans(X), "-")`
- Scaling: various normalization methods
- Block-wise transformations with index mapping

### 7. `/Users/bbuchsbaum/code/multivarious/R/all_generic.R` - Generic Functions
**Main Classes/Structures:**
- Extensive S3 generic definitions

**Key Exported Functions:**
- 39 generic functions including `project()`, `reconstruct()`, `perm_test()`, etc.

**Dependencies:**
- cli (for error messages)

**Complexity Level:** **Low-Medium**
- Primarily method dispatch definitions
- Comprehensive documentation
- Well-structured generic system

## Overall Package Complexity Assessment

**Complexity Distribution:**
- **High Complexity (3 files):** projector.R, pca.R, multiblock.R
- **Medium-High Complexity (2 files):** bi_projector.R, pre_process.R  
- **Medium Complexity (1 file):** svd.R
- **Low-Medium Complexity (1 file):** all_generic.R

**Key Technical Challenges for R→Rust Conversion:**
1. **Environment-based caching** (projector.R, pre_process.R)
2. **Complex S3 method dispatch** (throughout)
3. **Advanced statistical algorithms** (pca.R permutation testing)
4. **Dynamic memory management** (caching, environments)
5. **Block-wise operations** (multiblock.R, pre_process.R)
6. **Optional dependencies** (multiple SVD backends)

**Recommended Conversion Priority:**
1. Start with `svd.R` and `all_generic.R` (lowest complexity)
2. Build up through `bi_projector.R` and core `projector.R`
3. Tackle `pre_process.R` (environment challenges)
4. Handle `multiblock.R` and `pca.R` last (highest complexity)

The package demonstrates sophisticated statistical computing patterns with extensive use of S3 object orientation, environment-based state management, and complex mathematical algorithms suitable for a challenging but rewarding Rust conversion project.

# Feature Parity Analysis: R multivarious → Rust

## Overview

This document provides a comprehensive analysis of the R `multivarious` package (v0.2.0) to guide its conversion to idiomatic Rust. The package provides extensible data structures for multivariate analysis, including dimensionality reduction, projection methods, and preprocessing functions.

## Package Statistics

- **Total Exported Functions**: 115 (from NAMESPACE analysis)
- **Core R Files**: 21 files in `/R` directory
- **Dependencies**: 25 R packages (17 hard dependencies, 8 suggestions)
- **Lines of Code**: ~8,000+ lines across all R files
- **S3 Methods**: 106 S3 method implementations
- **Generic Functions**: 39 generic function definitions

## Core Architecture Analysis

### 1. Type System Mapping

| R Concept | Current R Implementation | Rust Target | Complexity |
|-----------|-------------------------|-------------|------------|
| S3 Classes | Lists with `class` attribute | `struct` with `impl` blocks | Medium |
| S3 Method Dispatch | `UseMethod()` calls | Trait objects/generics | High |
| R Environments | `new.env()` for caching | `HashMap` or `BTreeMap` | Medium |
| R Lists | Heterogeneous lists | `enum` or `Vec<T>` | Low-Medium |
| R Matrices | Base R `matrix` | `ndarray::Array2<f64>` | Low |
| R Data Frames | Not heavily used | `polars::DataFrame` | Low |

### 2. Core Classes and Features

#### 2.1 Base Projector System (`projector.R`)
**Current R Features:**
- Generic projection interface with `project()`, `reconstruct()`, `transpose()`
- Environment-based caching system for computed results
- Support for partial projections on column subsets
- Ridge-regularized least squares solutions
- Inverse projection calculations

**Rust Implementation Strategy:**
- Use traits for `Project`, `Reconstruct`, `Transpose` behavior
- Replace environment caching with `RefCell<HashMap<String, T>>` or `OnceCell`
- Implement ridge regression using `ndarray-linalg`
- Use `Result<T, E>` for all fallible operations

**Translation Risk**: **High** (complex caching, multiple projection modes)

#### 2.2 Bidirectional Projector (`bi_projector.R`)
**Current R Features:**
- Two-way mapping between sample and variable spaces
- SVD-based decomposition storage (`v`, `s`, `sdev`)
- Score computation and variable projection
- Reconstruction from both sample and variable perspectives

**Rust Implementation Strategy:**
```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct BiProjector {
    pub v: Array2<f64>,        // Variable loadings
    pub s: Array2<f64>,        // Scores  
    pub sdev: Array1<f64>,     // Standard deviations
    pub preproc: Option<Box<dyn Preprocessor>>,
}

impl Project for BiProjector { /* ... */ }
impl Reconstruct for BiProjector { /* ... */ }
```

**Translation Risk**: **Medium** (builds on projector complexity)

#### 2.3 Principal Component Analysis (`pca.R`)
**Current R Features:**
- Standard PCA with multiple SVD backends
- Permutation testing using Vitale et al. (2017) methodology
- Component rotation (varimax, promax, quartimax, etc.)
- Enhanced biplots with `ggplot2` and `ggrepel`
- Outlier detection with Hotelling T² and Q-residuals
- Bootstrap confidence intervals

**Rust Implementation Strategy:**
- Use `ndarray` with BLAS backend for SVD
- Implement permutation testing with `rayon` for parallelization
- Factor rotation using external crate or custom implementation
- Plotting with `plotters` or export to external tools
- Statistical testing with custom probability distributions

**Translation Risk**: **High** (most complex component, advanced statistics)

#### 2.4 SVD Wrapper (`svd.R`)
**Current R Features:**
- Unified interface for multiple SVD algorithms:
  - Base R `svd()`
  - `RSpectra::svds()` (sparse/truncated)
  - `irlba::irlba()` (iterative)
  - `rsvd::rsvd()` (randomized)
  - `corpcor::fast.svd()` (fast)
  - `svd::propack.svd()` (PROPACK)

**Rust Implementation Strategy:**
```rust
pub enum SvdMethod {
    Standard,
    Truncated(usize),  // number of components
    Randomized(usize),
    Iterative(usize),
}

pub trait SvdSolver {
    fn solve(&self, matrix: &Array2<f64>) -> Result<SvdResult, SvdError>;
}
```

**Translation Risk**: **Medium** (method dispatch, optional backends)

#### 2.5 Multiblock Analysis (`multiblock.R`)
**Current R Features:**
- Multi-block projector with block-wise operations
- Consensus testing via eigenvalue analysis
- Cross-block correlation computations
- Sequential component testing with stopping rules
- Block index management and mapping

**Rust Implementation Strategy:**
```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct MultiblockProjector {
    pub blocks: Vec<BlockInfo>,
    pub consensus_matrix: Array2<f64>,
    pub block_weights: Array1<f64>,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct BlockInfo {
    pub indices: Range<usize>,
    pub name: String,
    pub projector: Box<dyn Project>,
}
```

**Translation Risk**: **High** (complex consensus algorithms, block management)

#### 2.6 Preprocessing (`pre_process.R`)
**Current R Features:**
- Pipeline-based preprocessing with `prepper` → `pre_processor`
- Environment-based parameter storage
- Operations: centering, scaling, standardization
- Block-wise preprocessing for multiblock data
- Forward and inverse transformations
- Modern API: `fit()`, `fit_transform()`, `transform()`, `inverse_transform()`

**Rust Implementation Strategy:**
```rust
pub trait Preprocessor: Send + Sync {
    fn fit(&mut self, data: &Array2<f64>) -> Result<(), PreprocessError>;
    fn transform(&self, data: &Array2<f64>) -> Result<Array2<f64>, PreprocessError>;
    fn inverse_transform(&self, data: &Array2<f64>) -> Result<Array2<f64>, PreprocessError>;
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct PreprocessPipeline {
    steps: Vec<Box<dyn Preprocessor>>,
}
```

**Translation Risk**: **Medium-High** (environment storage, complex pipeline)

## Dependencies Analysis

### 2.7 Hard Dependencies Mapping

| R Package | Rust Equivalent | Usage in multivarious | Migration Strategy |
|-----------|----------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| `Matrix` | `sprs` or `nalgebra-sparse` | Sparse matrix operations | Use `sprs` for sparse linear algebra |
| `rlang` | Native Rust | NSE and metaprogramming | Eliminate through strong typing |
| `chk` | Custom validation | Input validation | Implement with `Result<T, E>` |
| `glmnet` | `linfa-elasticnet` | Regularized regression | Use ML ecosystem |
| `corpcor` | `ndarray-linalg` | Fast SVD, pseudoinverse | Core linear algebra |
| `rsvd` | Custom implementation | Randomized SVD | Implement using `ndarray` |
| `svd` | `ndarray-linalg` | PROPACK SVD | Use LAPACK backend |
| `pls` | `linfa-pls` | Partial least squares | Use ML ecosystem |
| `irlba` | Custom implementation | Iterative SVD | Port algorithm |
| `RSpectra` | `arpack` | Sparse eigenvalues | Use ARPACK binding |
| `matrixStats` | `ndarray-stats` | Matrix statistics | Statistical operations |
| `ggplot2`/`ggrepel` | `plotters` | Visualization | Export data for external plotting |
| `future.apply` | `rayon` | Parallel processing | Use data parallelism |
| `tibble`/`dplyr` | `polars` | Data manipulation | DataFrame operations |
| `MASS` | `linfa-linear` | Linear/quadratic discriminant | Use ML ecosystem |
| `geigen` | `ndarray-linalg` | Generalized eigenproblems | LAPACK routines |
| `PRIMME` | Custom implementation | Advanced eigensolvers | Complex port |
| `GPArotation` | Custom implementation | Factor rotation | Mathematical algorithms |

### 2.8 Suggested Dependencies (Optional Features)

| R Package | Rust Feature Flag | Purpose |
|-----------|------------------|---------|
| `randomForest` | `random-forest` | RF classifier wrapper |
| `testthat` | Built-in testing | Unit tests |
| `knitr`/`rmarkdown` | Documentation | Examples and vignettes |

## Complexity Assessment by Component

### High Complexity Components (Requires Expert Implementation)
1. **PCA Permutation Testing** (`pca.R:395-600`)
   - P3 projection methodology
   - Sequential hypothesis testing  
   - Complex statistical algorithms
   - **Estimated Effort**: 3-4 weeks

2. **Multiblock Consensus Testing** (`multiblock.R:400-550`)
   - Eigenvalue-based consensus metrics
   - Cross-block correlation analysis
   - **Estimated Effort**: 2-3 weeks

3. **Environment-based Caching** (`projector.R:100-200`)
   - Dynamic result caching
   - Memory management
   - **Estimated Effort**: 1-2 weeks

### Medium Complexity Components
1. **Factor Rotation** (`pca.R:200-350`)
   - Mathematical rotation algorithms
   - **Estimated Effort**: 1-2 weeks

2. **Preprocessing Pipeline** (`pre_process.R:1-400`)
   - Multi-step transformations
   - **Estimated Effort**: 1 week

3. **SVD Method Dispatch** (`svd.R:1-200`)
   - Multiple backend support
   - **Estimated Effort**: 1 week

### Low Complexity Components
1. **Basic Linear Algebra** (throughout)
   - Matrix operations, projections
   - **Estimated Effort**: 2-3 days each

2. **S3 Method Structure** (`all_generic.R`)
   - Trait definitions
   - **Estimated Effort**: 1 week total

## Performance Targets

Based on R2Rust conversion guide benchmarks:

| Operation | R Performance Baseline | Rust Target | Expected Improvement |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| Matrix multiplication | Reference | 2-5x faster | SIMD + zero-cost abstractions |
| SVD computation | Reference | 1.5-3x faster | Optimized BLAS |
| Preprocessing pipeline | Reference | 3-10x faster | No interpreter overhead |
| Memory usage | Reference | 50-80% reduction | No GC, explicit management |
| Cold start time | Reference | 100x faster | No interpreter loading |

## Risk Assessment

### High Risk Areas
1. **Permutation Testing Algorithms** - Complex statistical procedures
2. **S3 Method Dispatch** - R's dynamic dispatch vs Rust's static system
3. **Environment-based State** - R environments vs Rust ownership
4. **Optional Dependencies** - Runtime feature selection vs compile-time

### Medium Risk Areas  
1. **Error Handling** - R's warning system vs Rust's Result types
2. **Memory Layout** - R's column-major vs potential row-major optimization
3. **Parallel Processing** - R's future vs Rust's rayon differences

### Low Risk Areas
1. **Core Linear Algebra** - Well-established algorithms
2. **Basic Data Structures** - Straightforward mappings
3. **Input Validation** - Better with Rust's type system

## Testing Strategy

### 1. Cross-Language Validation
- Generate test fixtures from R using identical inputs
- Compare outputs using `approx::assert_relative_eq!` with ε=1e-12
- Test edge cases: empty matrices, singular matrices, etc.

### 2. Property-Based Testing  
- Use `proptest` for invariant checking
- Test mathematical properties: orthogonality, reconstruction accuracy
- Verify transformations are invertible where applicable

### 3. Performance Benchmarking
- Use `criterion` for Rust benchmarks
- Compare against R baselines using identical datasets
- Profile memory usage and computational complexity

## Success Criteria

### Functional Parity
- [ ] 100% of exported R functions implemented
- [ ] All S3 methods have Rust trait equivalents  
- [ ] Cross-validation tests pass within numerical tolerance
- [ ] Error cases handled appropriately

### Performance Targets
- [ ] Core operations ≥2x faster than R
- [ ] Memory usage ≤50% of R equivalent
- [ ] Cold start time ≤10ms vs R's ~1s
- [ ] Parallel scaling efficiency ≥80%

### Code Quality
- [ ] Zero `unsafe` code (or fully documented unsafe blocks)
- [ ] 100% documentation coverage with examples
- [ ] Passes `cargo clippy` with no warnings
- [ ] >95% test coverage

### Ecosystem Integration
- [ ] Published on crates.io
- [ ] Compatible with ndarray/polars ecosystem
- [ ] Optional Python bindings via PyO3
- [ ] Performance benchmarks published

## Conclusion

The `multivarious` package represents a sophisticated statistical computing library with moderate to high conversion complexity. The most challenging aspects will be:

1. **Complex statistical algorithms** (permutation testing, consensus analysis)
2. **Dynamic dispatch patterns** (S3 method system)
3. **Environment-based state management** (caching systems)

However, the conversion offers significant opportunities for performance improvements and type safety enhancements. The modular design suggests a phased implementation approach, starting with core linear algebra components and building up to advanced statistical procedures.

**Estimated Total Effort**: 3-4 months for a skilled Rust developer with statistics background
**Recommended Team Size**: 1-2 developers  
**Success Probability**: High (well-defined mathematical algorithms, good test coverage possible)

# Implementation Plan: R multivarious → Rust

## Overview

This document outlines a detailed, phased implementation plan for converting the R `multivarious` package to idiomatic Rust. The plan follows the R2Rust conversion guide and prioritizes incremental development with continuous validation.

## Project Setup

### Phase 0: Foundation Setup (Week 1)

#### T001: Repository Initialization
- [ ] Initialize Rust project with `cargo init`
- [ ] Set up project structure following R2Rust guide
- [ ] Configure `Cargo.toml` with initial dependencies
- [ ] Create documentation structure with `mdbook`
- [ ] Set up CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions)
- [ ] Configure development tools (clippy, fmt, tarpaulin)

**Files**: `Cargo.toml`, `.github/workflows/`, `README.md`
**Assignee**: Setup Lead
**Estimated**: 2 days

#### T002: Core Dependencies and Build System
- [ ] Add core dependencies: `ndarray`, `ndarray-linalg`, `thiserror`, `serde`
- [ ] Set up feature flags for optional components
- [ ] Configure release profile for optimization
- [ ] Set up benchmark infrastructure with `criterion`
- [ ] Create development scripts and documentation

**Dependencies**: `ndarray = "0.15"`, `ndarray-linalg = "0.16"`, `thiserror = "1.0"`, `criterion = "0.5"`
**Estimated**: 1 day

#### T003: Error Handling and Common Types
- [ ] Define comprehensive error types using `thiserror`
- [ ] Create common type aliases (`Matrix`, `Vector`, etc.)
- [ ] Implement basic validation utilities
- [ ] Set up logging infrastructure
- [ ] Create testing utilities and fixtures

**Files**: `src/errors.rs`, `src/types.rs`, `src/utils.rs`
**Priority**: Critical
**Estimated**: 2 days

```rust
// Core error types
#[derive(thiserror::Error, Debug)]
pub enum MultivariousError {
    #[error("Dimension mismatch: expected {expected}, got {actual}")]
```

## Risk Mitigation

### High Risk Components - Additional Planning

#### Statistical Algorithm Validation
- **Risk**: Complex statistical procedures may not match R exactly
- **Mitigation**: Implement reference implementations first, extensive cross-validation
- **Timeline**: Add 20% buffer to T313, T412

#### Memory Management Complexity  
- **Risk**: Caching system complexity may cause ownership issues
- **Mitigation**: Start with simple `HashMap` caching, optimize later
- **Timeline**: Prototype caching early in T202

#### Dependency Integration
- **Risk**: External crate compatibility issues
- **Mitigation**: Evaluate alternatives early, consider vendoring critical components
- **Timeline**: Dependency evaluation in T002

## Resource Requirements

### Development Environment
- Rust 1.70+ with nightly for benchmarking
- R 4.0+ for cross-validation
- Linux/macOS for BLAS/LAPACK integration
- 16GB+ RAM for large matrix testing
- Fast SSD for compilation performance

### External Dependencies
- BLAS/LAPACK libraries (OpenBLAS, Intel MKL)
- Optional: ARPACK for sparse eigenproblems
- Testing: R installation with multivarious package

## Success Metrics

### Functional Completeness
- [ ] All 115 exported R functions have Rust equivalents
- [ ] Cross-validation tests pass within 1e-12 tolerance
- [ ] Property-based tests pass for all mathematical invariants
- [ ] Documentation coverage >95%

### Performance Targets
- [ ] Matrix operations: 2-5x faster than R
- [ ] Memory usage: <50% of R equivalent  
- [ ] Cold start: <10ms vs R's ~1s
- [ ] Parallel efficiency: >80% scaling

### Code Quality
- [ ] Zero unsafe code (or fully documented)
- [ ] Clippy warnings: zero
- [ ] Test coverage: >95%
- [ ] Compile time: <2 minutes for full build

## Timeline Summary

**Total Duration**: 17 weeks (4.25 months)
**Critical Path**: T003 → T201 → T202 → T211 → T301 → T313
**Parallel Work Opportunities**: Documentation, testing, optimization
**Buffer Time**: 2 weeks for complex statistical components

**Key Milestones**:
- Week 3: Core linear algebra functional
- Week 6: Basic PCA working
- Week 9: Advanced PCA features complete
- Week 12: Multiblock analysis functional
- Week 16: Full cross-validation passing
- Week 17: Package ready for publication

This implementation plan provides a structured approach to converting the R multivarious package to Rust while maintaining mathematical correctness and achieving significant performance improvements.

This document outlines a detailed, phased implementation plan for converting the R `multivarious` package to idiomatic Rust. The plan follows the R2Rust conversion guide and prioritizes incremental development with continuous validation.

## Project Setup

**Files**: `src/analysis/pca/selection.rs`
**Depends**: T302
**Estimated**: 3 days

### T310: Advanced PCA Features

#### T311: Component Rotation System
- [ ] Design rotation trait system (`Rotation`, `OrthogonalRotation`)
- [ ] Implement Varimax rotation algorithm
- [ ] Add Promax rotation (oblique)
- [ ] Implement Quartimax rotation
- [ ] Add rotation convergence criteria

**Files**: `src/analysis/pca/rotation/mod.rs`, `src/analysis/pca/rotation/varimax.rs`
**Depends**: T302
**Estimated**: 5 days

```rust
pub trait Rotation {
    fn rotate(&self, loadings: &Array2<f64>) -> Result<RotationResult, MultivariousError>;
}

pub struct VarimaxRotation {
    max_iter: usize,
    tolerance: f64,
}
```

#### T312: PCA Diagnostics
- [ ] Implement outlier detection (Hotelling T², Q-residuals)
- [ ] Add influence measures for observations
- [ ] Implement contribution analysis for variables
- [ ] Add model diagnostics and fit statistics
- [ ] Validation against R diagnostic methods

**Files**: `src/analysis/pca/diagnostics.rs`
**Depends**: T302
**Estimated**: 4 days

#### T313: PCA Permutation Testing (Complex)
- [ ] Implement Vitale et al. (2017) permutation methodology
- [ ] Add P3 projection for sequential testing
- [ ] Implement parallel permutation execution with `rayon`
- [ ] Add sequential stopping rules with alpha control
- [ ] Statistical result structures and reporting

**Files**: `src/analysis/pca/permutation.rs`
**Depends**: T302
**Priority**: High complexity - requires statistical expertise
**Estimated**: 8 days

```rust
pub struct PermutationTest {
    pub nperm: usize,
    pub alpha: f64,
    pub parallel: bool,
}

impl PermutationTest {
    pub fn test_pca(&self, pca: &Pca, data: &Array2<f64>) 
        -> Result<PermutationResult, MultivariousError> {
        // Complex statistical implementation
    }
}
```

## Phase 4: Multiblock Analysis (Weeks 10-12)

### T400: Multiblock Infrastructure

#### T401: Block Management System
- [ ] Design `BlockInfo` and `BlockIndex` structures
- [ ] Implement block splitting and indexing utilities
- [ ] Add block name management and validation
- [ ] Create block-wise operation helpers
- [ ] Testing with various block configurations

**Files**: `src/multiblock/blocks.rs`
**Depends**: T003
**Estimated**: 3 days

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct BlockInfo {
    pub name: String,
    pub indices: std::ops::Range<usize>,
    pub length: usize,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct BlockManager {
    blocks: Vec<BlockInfo>,
    total_variables: usize,
}
```

#### T402: Multiblock Projector Base
- [ ] Design `MultiblockProjector` with block awareness
- [ ] Implement `project_block()` for single block projection
- [ ] Add block-wise preprocessing support
- [ ] Implement block indices and lengths accessors
- [ ] Cross-validation with R `multiblock_projector`

**Files**: `src/multiblock/projector.rs`
**Depends**: T401, T202
**Estimated**: 4 days

### T410: Multiblock Biprojector

#### T411: Consensus Analysis Foundation
- [ ] Design `MultiblockBiProjector` structure
- [ ] Implement consensus matrix computation
- [ ] Add block weight calculation
- [ ] Implement cross-block score correlation
- [ ] Basic multiblock decomposition

**Files**: `src/multiblock/biprojector.rs`
**Depends**: T402, T211
**Estimated**: 5 days

#### T412: Advanced Consensus Testing
- [ ] Implement eigenvalue-based consensus statistics
- [ ] Add sequential component testing framework
- [ ] Implement RSpectra integration for sparse eigenproblems
- [ ] Add stopping rule implementation with alpha control
- [ ] Parallel execution for permutation testing

**Files**: `src/multiblock/consensus.rs`
**Depends**: T411
**Priority**: High complexity - advanced multivariate statistics
**Estimated**: 7 days

## Phase 5: Classification and Regression (Weeks 13-14)

### T500: Discriminant Analysis

#### T501: Discriminant Projector
- [ ] Design `DiscriminantProjector` with class information
- [ ] Implement LDA-based discriminant analysis
- [ ] Add prediction methods (LDA and Euclidean)
- [ ] Implement cross-validation for classification
- [ ] Integration with external ML libraries

**Files**: `src/analysis/discriminant.rs`
**Depends**: T211
**Estimated**: 4 days

#### T502: Classification Framework
- [ ] Design generic `Classifier` trait
- [ ] Implement prediction and scoring methods
- [ ] Add feature importance calculation
- [ ] Implement random forest wrapper (optional)
- [ ] Performance metrics and evaluation

**Files**: `src/classification/mod.rs`
**Depends**: T501
**Estimated**: 3 days

### T510: Regression Analysis

#### T511: Cross Projector (CCA-style)
- [ ] Implement cross-decomposition between X and Y blocks
- [ ] Add canonical correlation analysis
- [ ] Implement transfer functions between domains
- [ ] Add reconstruction error measures
- [ ] Cross-validation with R implementations

**Files**: `src/analysis/cross_projector.rs`
**Depends**: T211
**Estimated**: 5 days

## Phase 6: Testing and Validation (Weeks 15-16)

### T600: Cross-Language Validation

#### T601: Test Infrastructure
- [ ] Create R script for generating test fixtures
- [ ] Implement JSON serialization for test data
- [ ] Set up automated cross-validation pipeline
- [ ] Add numerical tolerance testing with `approx`
- [ ] Performance comparison framework

**Files**: `tests/fixtures/`, `scripts/generate_fixtures.R`
**Priority**: Critical for correctness validation
**Estimated**: 3 days

#### T602: Comprehensive Test Suite
- [ ] Unit tests for all core components (>95% coverage)
- [ ] Integration tests for complete workflows
- [ ] Property-based tests with `proptest`
- [ ] Edge case testing (empty matrices, singular cases)
- [ ] Performance regression tests

**Files**: `tests/integration/`, `tests/property/`
**Estimated**: 5 days

#### T603: Documentation and Examples
- [ ] Complete rustdoc documentation with examples
- [ ] Create comprehensive user guide with `mdbook`
- [ ] Port R vignettes to Rust examples
- [ ] Add performance benchmarks and comparisons
- [ ] Migration guide from R

**Files**: `docs/`, `examples/`
**Estimated**: 4 days

## Phase 7: Optimization and Packaging (Week 17)

### T700: Performance Optimization

#### T701: SIMD and Parallelization
- [ ] Profile code with `flamegraph` and `perf`
- [ ] Optimize hot paths with SIMD instructions
- [ ] Add parallel processing with `rayon` where beneficial
- [ ] Memory layout optimization for cache efficiency
- [ ] Benchmark against performance targets

**Estimated**: 3 days

#### T702: Final Packaging
- [ ] Prepare for crates.io publication
- [ ] Optimize release build configuration
- [ ] Add comprehensive CI/CD pipeline
- [ ] Create release documentation
- [ ] Performance benchmarks publication

**Estimated**: 2 days

## Risk Mitigation

### High Risk Components - Additional Planning

#### Statistical Algorithm Validation
- **Risk**: Complex statistical procedures may not match R exactly
- **Mitigation**: Implement reference implementations first, extensive cross-validation
- **Timeline**: Add 20% buffer to T313, T412

#### Memory Management Complexity  
- **Risk**: Caching system complexity may cause ownership issues
- **Mitigation**: Start with simple `HashMap` caching, optimize later
- **Timeline**: Prototype caching early in T202

#### Dependency Integration
- **Risk**: External crate compatibility issues
- **Mitigation**: Evaluate alternatives early, consider vendoring critical components
- **Timeline**: Dependency evaluation in T002

## Resource Requirements

### Development Environment
- Rust 1.70+ with nightly for benchmarking
- R 4.0+ for cross-validation
- Linux/macOS for BLAS/LAPACK integration
- 16GB+ RAM for large matrix testing
- Fast SSD for compilation performance

### External Dependencies
- BLAS/LAPACK libraries (OpenBLAS, Intel MKL)
- Optional: ARPACK for sparse eigenproblems
- Testing: R installation with multivarious package

## Success Metrics

### Functional Completeness
- [ ] All 115 exported R functions have Rust equivalents
- [ ] Cross-validation tests pass within 1e-12 tolerance
- [ ] Property-based tests pass for all mathematical invariants
- [ ] Documentation coverage >95%

### Performance Targets
- [ ] Matrix operations: 2-5x faster than R
- [ ] Memory usage: <50% of R equivalent  
- [ ] Cold start: <10ms vs R's ~1s
- [ ] Parallel efficiency: >80% scaling

### Code Quality
- [ ] Zero unsafe code (or fully documented)
- [ ] Clippy warnings: zero
- [ ] Test coverage: >95%
- [ ] Compile time: <2 minutes for full build

## Timeline Summary

**Total Duration**: 17 weeks (4.25 months)
**Critical Path**: T003 → T201 → T202 → T211 → T301 → T313
**Parallel Work Opportunities**: Documentation, testing, optimization
**Buffer Time**: 2 weeks for complex statistical components

**Key Milestones**:
- Week 3: Core linear algebra functional
- Week 6: Basic PCA working
- Week 9: Advanced PCA features complete
- Week 12: Multiblock analysis functional
- Week 16: Full cross-validation passing
- Week 17: Package ready for publication

This implementation plan provides a structured approach to converting the R multivarious package to Rust while maintaining mathematical correctness and achieving significant performance improvements.

# Progress Tracking: R multivarious → Rust Implementation

## Current Status: 📋 Planning Complete

**Last Updated**: 2025-07-12  
**Overall Progress**: 0% implementation, 100% planning  
**Current Phase**: Phase 0 - Foundation Setup  
**Next Tasks**: T001 - Repository Initialization

---

## Phase Overview

| Phase | Name | Status | Progress | Start Date | Target Date | Actual Date |
|-------|------|--------|----------|------------|-------------|-------------|
| 0 | Foundation Setup | 🔄 Ready | 0% | 2025-07-12 | 2025-07-19 | - |
| 1 | Core Linear Algebra | ⏳ Pending | 0% | 2025-07-20 | 2025-08-02 | - |
| 2 | Core Projection System | ⏳ Pending | 0% | 2025-08-03 | 2025-08-23 | - |
| 3 | PCA Implementation | ⏳ Pending | 0% | 2025-08-24 | 2025-09-13 | - |
| 4 | Multiblock Analysis | ⏳ Pending | 0% | 2025-09-14 | 2025-10-04 | - |
| 5 | Classification/Regression | ⏳ Pending | 0% | 2025-10-05 | 2025-10-18 | - |
| 6 | Testing and Validation | ⏳ Pending | 0% | 2025-10-19 | 2025-11-01 | - |
| 7 | Optimization/Packaging | ⏳ Pending | 0% | 2025-11-02 | 2025-11-08 | - |

**Legend**: ✅ Complete, 🔄 In Progress, ⏳ Pending, ⚠️ Blocked, ❌ Failed

---

## Current Sprint: Phase 0 - Foundation Setup

### Week 1 Tasks (July 12-19, 2025)

#### 🎯 T001: Repository Initialization
**Status**: ⏳ Not Started  
**Priority**: Critical  
**Estimated**: 2 days  
**Dependencies**: None  

**Checklist**:
- [ ] Initialize Rust project with `cargo init`
- [ ] Set up project structure following R2Rust guide
- [ ] Configure `Cargo.toml` with initial dependencies
- [ ] Create documentation structure with `mdbook`
- [ ] Set up CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions)
- [ ] Configure development tools (clippy, fmt, tarpaulin)

**Files to Create**:
- [ ] `Cargo.toml`
- [ ] `src/lib.rs`
- [ ] `.github/workflows/ci.yml`
- [ ] `README.md`
- [ ] `docs/book.toml`

---

#### 🔧 T002: Core Dependencies and Build System
**Status**: ⏳ Not Started  
**Priority**: Critical  
**Estimated**: 1 day  
**Dependencies**: T001  

**Checklist**:
- [ ] Add core dependencies: `ndarray`, `ndarray-linalg`, `thiserror`, `serde`
- [ ] Set up feature flags for optional components
- [ ] Configure release profile for optimization
- [ ] Set up benchmark infrastructure with `criterion`
- [ ] Create development scripts and documentation

**Target Dependencies**:
```toml
[dependencies]
ndarray = "0.15"
ndarray-linalg = "0.16"
thiserror = "1.0"
serde = { version = "1.0", features = ["derive"] }
num-traits = "0.2"
rayon = { version = "1.8", optional = true }

[dev-dependencies]
criterion = { version = "0.5", features = ["html_reports"] }
approx = "0.5"
tempfile = "3.8"
```

---

#### 🎨 T003: Error Handling and Common Types
**Status**: ⏳ Not Started  
**Priority**: Critical  
**Estimated**: 2 days  
**Dependencies**: T002  

**Checklist**:
- [ ] Define comprehensive error types using `thiserror`
- [ ] Create common type aliases (`Matrix`, `Vector`, etc.)
- [ ] Implement basic validation utilities
- [ ] Set up logging infrastructure
- [ ] Create testing utilities and fixtures

**Key Files**:
- [ ] `src/errors.rs`
- [ ] `src/types.rs`
- [ ] `src/utils.rs`
- [ ] `src/validation.rs`

**Error Types to Implement**:
- [ ] `DimensionMismatch`
- [ ] `LinAlgError`
- [ ] `InvalidComponentCount`
- [ ] `SingularMatrix`
- [ ] `ConvergenceError`

---

## Ready for Implementation

### Next Up: T100 Series - SVD Wrapper (Week 2)

The following tasks are ready to begin once Phase 0 is complete:

#### T101: Basic SVD Interface (3 days)
- Design `SvdMethod` enum and `SvdSolver` trait
- Implement standard SVD using `ndarray-linalg`
- **Validation Target**: Match R `svd()` on iris dataset

#### T102: Advanced SVD Methods (4 days)  
- Implement truncated and randomized SVD
- **Validation Target**: Compare with R `RSpectra::svds()` and `rsvd::rsvd()`

#### T103: SVD Result Structure (2 days)
- Create comprehensive result structure
- **Validation Target**: Cross-validate with R `svd_wrapper()`

---

## Key Metrics Dashboard

### Implementation Progress
- **Total Tasks**: 43 planned
- **Completed Tasks**: 0
- **In Progress**: 0  
- **Blocked Tasks**: 0
- **Lines of Code**: 0 (target: ~15,000)

- **Test Coverage**: 0% (target: >95%)
- **Documentation Coverage**: 0% (target: >95%)
- **Clippy Warnings**: 0 (target: 0)
- **Build Time**: N/A (target: <2 minutes)

### Performance Targets
- **Matrix Operations**: N/A (target: 2-5x faster than R)
- **Memory Usage**: N/A (target: <50% of R)
- **Cold Start**: N/A (target: <10ms vs R's ~1s)

---

## Risk Tracking

### Current Risks
1. **🟡 Complexity of Statistical Algorithms** (Medium Risk)
   - **Impact**: Permutation testing and multiblock consensus may be complex
   - **Mitigation**: Plan additional time for T313 and T412
   - **Status**: Monitoring

2. **🟡 Dependency Integration** (Medium Risk)
   - **Impact**: BLAS/LAPACK integration challenges
   - **Mitigation**: Early testing with multiple backends
   - **Status**: Monitoring

### Resolved Risks
- None yet

---

## Blockers and Issues

### Current Blockers
- None

### Recent Issues
- None

---

## Resource Allocation

### Current Assignments
- **Planning Phase**: Complete ✅
- **Implementation**: Ready to begin

### Required Resources
- **Development Environment**: Rust 1.70+, R 4.0+, 16GB+ RAM
- **External Dependencies**: BLAS/LAPACK libraries
- **Time Allocation**: 17 weeks (4.25 months) estimated

---

## Communication Log

### Recent Updates
- **2025-07-12**: Planning phase completed
  - ✅ Feature parity analysis finished
  - ✅ Implementation plan with 43 granular tasks created
  - ✅ Progress tracking system established
  - 🎯 Ready to begin T001 - Repository Initialization

### Upcoming Milestones
- **Week 3 Target**: Core linear algebra functional
- **Week 6 Target**: Basic PCA working  
- **Week 9 Target**: Advanced PCA features complete
- **Week 12 Target**: Multiblock analysis functional
- **Week 16 Target**: Full cross-validation passing
- **Week 17 Target**: Package ready for publication

---

## Quick Actions

### Ready to Start Today
1. **T001**: Initialize Cargo project and basic structure
2. **T002**: Set up core dependencies in `Cargo.toml`
3. **T003**: Define error types and basic utilities

### This Week's Goals
- Complete Phase 0 foundation setup
- Have working Rust project with CI/CD
- Basic error handling and types in place
- Ready to begin SVD implementation next week

### Need Help With
- None currently - all tasks are well-defined and ready for implementation

---

*This progress document will be updated weekly throughout the implementation. Each completed task will be marked with completion date and any notes about implementation decisions or challenges encountered.*

content.text:
## Conversion Plan Complete ✅

I've successfully created a comprehensive plan for converting the R `multivarious` package to Rust with three detailed documents:

### 📊 [`feature_parity.md`](feature_parity.md)
**Comprehensive technical analysis including:**
- 115 exported R functions mapped to Rust equivalents
- Complexity assessment (High/Medium/Low risk components)
- Type system mapping (S3 classes → Rust traits/structs)
- Dependency analysis (25 R packages → Rust crates)
- Performance targets (2-5x speedup expected)

### 📋 [`plan.md`](plan.md) 
**Detailed 17-week implementation roadmap with 43 granular tickets:**
- **Phase 0**: Foundation setup (Week 1)
- **Phase 1**: Core linear algebra - SVD, preprocessing (Weeks 2-3) 
- **Phase 2**: Projection system - traits, base/bi-projectors (Weeks 4-6)
- **Phase 3**: PCA with rotation & permutation testing (Weeks 7-9)
- **Phase 4**: Multiblock consensus analysis (Weeks 10-12)
- **Phase 5**: Classification & regression (Weeks 13-14)
- **Phase 6**: Testing & validation (Weeks 15-16)
- **Phase 7**: Optimization & packaging (Week 17)

### 📈 [`progress.md`](progress.md)
**Living document for tracking implementation status:**
- Phase-by-phase progress tracking
- Task completion checklists  
- Risk monitoring & mitigation
- Performance metrics dashboard
- Weekly milestone tracking

## Key Highlights

**High Complexity Components** (requiring expert implementation):
- PCA permutation testing with P3 projection methodology
- Multiblock consensus testing with eigenvalue analysis
- Environment-based caching system conversion

**Expected Performance Gains**:
- Matrix operations: 2-5x faster
- Memory usage: 50-80% reduction  
- Cold start: 100x faster (no interpreter)

**Next Steps**: Ready to begin T001 - Repository Initialization. The plan provides clear guidance for converting this sophisticated statistical computing library while maintaining mathematical correctness and achieving significant performance improvements.
